claim: The zodiac section's anomalously low entropy (3.7149 bits, lowest of all sections) reflects a label-heavy encoding in which a large fraction of tokens are proper-name labels (star names, month names) encoded with a homophonic cipher, producing reduced glyph entropy relative to prose sections.
evidence_for: Zodiac section has lowest entropy of all sections (3.7149 bits) and only 1,297 words across 12 folios, consistent with sparse label text rather than continuous prose
Astronomical section also shows low entropy (3.7471 bits) and similarly sparse word count (872 words, 8 folios), consistent with label-dominated content
Homophonic substitution of a small fixed label vocabulary would reduce effective glyph entropy by concentrating probability mass on fewer glyph sequences
evidence_against: Low entropy could alternatively reflect a simpler or less redundant cipher rather than label-specific encoding
If zodiac labels encoded known medieval star/month names, partial decipherment via frequency matching would already have succeeded
Word length distribution in zodiac section would need to match typical medieval Latin star-name lengths (3-8 characters)
test: Compute type-token ratio and average word length distribution for zodiac and astronomical sections vs. herbal and recipes sections; test whether zodiac/astronomical show significantly higher type-token ratio (fewer repeated tokens) consistent with label lists, or lower (consistent with repeated formulaic labels)
test_metric: Type-token ratio per section, mean and variance of word length per section, proportion of hapax legomena per section
test_threshold: Confirmation if zodiac type-token ratio is significantly lower than herbal/recipes (p < 0.05), indicating repeated labels; denial if zodiac hapax rate matches or exceeds herbal section hapax rate

tests_run.test: compute type-token ratio and average word length distribution for zodiac and astronomical sections v
tests_run.details: Observed hapax ratio: 0.701. Expected: ~0.50. Voynich has unusually high hapax ratio (70.1%), suggesting either large vocabulary, verbose encoding, or noise.

reasoning: Label-dominated sections would exhibit a characteristic statistical fingerprint distinct from prose; confirming this would justify targeted decipherment attempts using known medieval astronomical nomenclature as a crib
